network.overall_strategy: A convergent prebiotic synthesis network for glycine integrating ten distinct pathways across hydrothermal, surface, and biochemical environments. The network starts from simple C1 gases (CO₂, CO, HCN, H₂CO) and nitrogen sources (NH₃, NO₃⁻), building through five hub molecules—ammonia, formaldehyde, formic acid, glyoxylate, and glycolaldehyde—that interconnect the pathways. Hydrothermal reactions on iron-sulfur and iron-oxide minerals fix CO₂ to formate and formaldehyde; surface reactions via Miller-Urey discharge, Strecker synthesis, formose chemistry, and formaldimine coupling generate key intermediates; and both environments feed glycine-producing terminal steps including reductive amination on ferroan brucite (Chimiak et al. 2024), Strecker hydrolysis, ethanolamine oxidation (Zhang et al. 2017), direct mineral surface catalysis (Mates-Torres & Rimola 2026), and formaldimine-formic acid coupling (Xu et al. 2024). Ethanolamine is explicitly synthesized from glycolaldehyde and ammonia via reductive amination, resolving the orphan intermediate problem from prior attempts.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO₂ reduction to formate on greigite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent fluid, pH 9-11, H₂-rich serpentinization effluent

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ is adsorbed on the Fe₃S₄ surface, which acts as an analog of [Fe₄S₄] clusters in modern acetyl-CoA pathway enzymes. H₂ dissociates on the mineral surface, providing hydrides that reduce CO₂ via a formate intermediate bound to the iron-sulfur surface. The reaction proceeds through a surface-bound carboxyl species that is hydrogenated to formate (HCOO⁻), which then desorbs into the alkaline fluid as formic acid/formate.
reactions.reasoning: CO₂ reduction to formate on iron-sulfur minerals is a well-established hydrothermal reaction. Greigite (Fe₃S₄) is structurally analogous to biological [4Fe-4S] clusters and has been demonstrated to catalyze CO₂ hydrogenation to formate under alkaline hydrothermal conditions. This is the first committed step in abiotic carbon fixation, analogous to the Wood-Ljungdahl pathway. Supported by multiple studies on vent mineralogy and CO₂ fixation (reviewed in Chimiak et al. 2024; also Preiner et al. 2020).

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type reduction of formate to formaldehyde

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch-type conditions in subseafloor hydrothermal systems; requires strong reductant Fe⁰ to drive the 2-electron reduction

reactions.mechanism: Formic acid adsorbs onto the magnetite/Fe⁰ surface. Elemental iron provides the reducing equivalents necessary for the 2-electron reduction of the carboxyl group. The reaction proceeds through a surface-bound formyl (HCO) intermediate: HCOOH + 2[H]_surface → H₂CO + H₂O. Fe⁰ is oxidized to Fe²⁺ in the process. Magnetite provides the catalytic surface geometry and Fe⁰ provides the thermodynamic driving force.
reactions.reasoning: The reduction of formate to formaldehyde is part of the Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) synthesis chain observed on magnetite and native iron under hydrothermal conditions. While formaldehyde is a relatively unstable intermediate, the high pressures and mineral surfaces in subseafloor systems can stabilize it long enough for downstream reactions. FTT synthesis on magnetite has been demonstrated to produce C1-C3 oxygenates including formaldehyde at 400-500K (reviewed in McCollom & Seewald 2007). The presence of Fe⁰ as a strong reductant is critical—without it, the reaction is thermodynamically unfavorable. Serpentinization systems naturally produce both magnetite and native iron.

reactions.name: Reverse water-gas shift: CO₂ to CO on magnetite

conditions.other: Black smoker or deep subseafloor conditions; thermodynamically favored at higher temperatures

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ + H₂ → CO + H₂O via the reverse water-gas shift reaction. On magnetite surfaces, CO₂ is activated by interaction with Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ sites. H₂ dissociatively adsorbs, and oxygen is transferred from CO₂ to form H₂O, releasing CO. This equilibrium shifts toward CO at higher temperatures (>450K).
reactions.reasoning: The reverse water-gas shift reaction is well established in heterogeneous catalysis and has been proposed as a key reaction in hydrothermal systems for generating CO from CO₂. Magnetite is an effective catalyst for this transformation. CO is required as a reactant in the direct mineral surface glycine synthesis (Mates-Torres & Rimola 2026) and is also relevant to Fischer-Tropsch chain growth. The reaction is thermodynamically favored at the high temperatures found in black smoker-type systems.

reactions.name: Formose-type aldol condensation of formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: Evaporitic pool or warm pond; alkaline pH ~10-11 from Ca(OH)₂; formaldehyde concentrated by evaporation; borate minerals may stabilize products

reactions.mechanism: Two molecules of formaldehyde undergo base-catalyzed aldol condensation. Ca(OH)₂ provides the alkaline conditions necessary to generate the enolate of formaldehyde (via Cannizzaro-type chemistry generating the glycolaldehyde anion). The first formaldehyde molecule is deprotonated to form a carbanion-like species, which attacks the carbonyl of the second formaldehyde. Montmorillonite clay surfaces concentrate the formaldehyde and template the reaction. 2 H₂CO → HOCH₂CHO.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction (Butlerow 1861) is one of the most established prebiotic sugar synthesis routes. The first step—dimerization of formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde—requires alkaline conditions and is catalyzed by Ca(OH)₂. Montmorillonite clay surfaces enhance the reaction by concentrating formaldehyde from dilute solutions and providing acid-base catalytic sites. Glycolaldehyde is a critical C₂ intermediate that feeds into both the glyoxylate pathway and ethanolamine synthesis. This reaction is well-documented in prebiotic chemistry (Breslow 1959; reviewed in Orgel 2000).

reactions.name: Oxidation of glycolaldehyde to glyoxylate on pyrite

conditions.other: Anoxic conditions; Fe³⁺ sites on pyrite surface act as electron acceptor; alkaline pH facilitates deprotonation of the alcohol group

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde (HOCH₂CHO) is adsorbed onto the pyrite surface via coordination of its hydroxyl and carbonyl oxygen atoms to Fe sites. The primary alcohol (–CH₂OH) is oxidized to a carboxyl group (–COOH) through sequential 2-electron oxidation steps, with Fe³⁺ surface sites accepting electrons (reduced to Fe²⁺). The product glyoxylic acid (OHC–COOH) retains the aldehyde functional group, making it reactive toward subsequent amination. HOCH₂CHO → OHC–COOH + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻.
reactions.reasoning: Pyrite surfaces are known to mediate oxidation reactions in hydrothermal systems, with surface Fe³⁺ acting as an electron acceptor (Wächtershäuser 1988). The oxidation of a primary alcohol to carboxylic acid is thermodynamically favorable when coupled to Fe³⁺ reduction. Glyoxylate is a key metabolic intermediate and the direct precursor to glycine via reductive amination in the Chimiak et al. (2024) pathway. This reaction connects the surface formose chemistry to the hydrothermal glyoxylate network.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of glyoxylate to glycine on ferroan brucite

conditions.other: pH 8-9; Fe(II)-rich alkaline mineral surface; NH₃ at 100 mM scale; weeks-long reaction in Chimiak experiments; ~52-69% yield from glyoxylate + NH₃ system

reactions.mechanism: Glyoxylate (OHC–COOH) condenses with NH₃ to form an imine intermediate (glyoxylamide/iminoglyoxylate: N=CH–COOH). This imine is then reduced by Fe(II) in the ferroan brucite lattice, which donates electrons: Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ + e⁻. The imine C=N bond is reduced to C–NH₂, yielding glycine (NH₂CH₂COOH). The brucite surface provides both the adsorption template and the reducing equivalents. Glycine strongly adsorbs to the mineral surface via its zwitterionic form.
reactions.reasoning: This is the central reaction of the Chimiak et al. (2024) study [Chimiak et al., PNAS 2024]. They demonstrated that glyoxylate (100 mM) reacted with NH₃ on ferroan brucite at ~20°C, yielding 52-69% glycine (by carbon conversion) over weeks. The Fe(II) in the brucite provides reducing equivalents for the reductive amination. This is one of the highest-yield prebiotic glycine syntheses reported. Notably, up to 97% of the glycine product was mineral-bound, detectable only after acid digestion. The mechanism parallels biological transamination and is fully consistent with alkaline vent geochemistry.

reactions.name: Miller-Urey electric discharge synthesis of HCN and formaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Electric discharge (lightning); TiO₂ photocatalysis as supplementary pathway
conditions.other: Atmospheric/gas phase reaction; reducing or weakly reducing atmosphere (CH₄, N₂/NH₃, H₂O); lightning or coronal discharge; products dissolve into surface water bodies

reactions.mechanism: Electric discharge dissociates CH₄, NH₃, and H₂O into radicals (·CH₃, ·NH₂, ·OH, ·H, ·CN). Recombination of carbon and nitrogen radicals produces HCN (·CH + ·N → HCN or via CH₂ + N → HCN + H). Formaldehyde forms from ·CH₃ + ·OH → CH₃OH → H₂CO or from CO + H₂ recombination. These products rain out into surface water bodies where they participate in aqueous chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Stanley Miller's classic 1953 experiment demonstrated that electric discharge through CH₄/NH₃/H₂O/H₂ produces amino acids, with glycine as a major product (Miller 1953). The key intermediates are HCN and H₂CO, which then undergo Strecker synthesis in the aqueous phase. Later work (e.g., Schlesinger & Miller 1983; Cleaves et al. 2008) confirmed these findings under various atmospheric compositions including CO/N₂ mixtures (0.1-5% carbon yields; Miyakawa et al. 2002). TiO₂ mineral photocatalysis is included as a supplementary radical generation mechanism on mineral surfaces exposed to UV.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis: aminoacetonitrile formation

conditions.other: Neutral to slightly acidic pH (5-7); warm pond or tidal flat; HCN, NH₃, and H₂CO concentrated by evaporation; montmorillonite concentrates reactants on interlayer surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Step 1: Formaldehyde (H₂CO) condenses with ammonia (NH₃) to form methyleneimine (formaldimine, CH₂=NH) with loss of water. Step 2: HCN attacks the electrophilic carbon of formaldimine in a nucleophilic addition, forming aminoacetonitrile (NH₂CH₂CN). The overall reaction is: H₂CO + NH₃ + HCN → NH₂CH₂CN + H₂O. Montmorillonite clay catalyzes by concentrating reactants in its interlayer spaces and providing Brønsted acid sites for imine formation.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is the most classical route to amino acids in prebiotic chemistry. First proposed by Strecker (1850) and demonstrated prebiotically by Miller (1953; 1957). The condensation of an aldehyde with ammonia and HCN to form an aminonitrile is the first step; hydrolysis then yields the amino acid. Aminoacetonitrile (NH₂CH₂CN) is the direct Strecker intermediate for glycine. Montmorillonite clay is specified as a catalyst because it is known to concentrate organics and catalyze condensation reactions in surface environments (Ferris et al. 1996).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycine

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite clay; mildly acidic pH or UV-driven hydrolysis
conditions.other: Warm evaporitic pool or volcanic hot spring; pH 4-7; may proceed through glycinamide intermediate; UV can accelerate hydrolysis; wet-dry cycles concentrate reactants

reactions.mechanism: Aminoacetonitrile (NH₂CH₂CN) undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: (1) Water attacks the nitrile carbon, forming an amide intermediate (glycinamide, NH₂CH₂CONH₂); (2) A second hydrolysis cleaves the amide to yield glycine (NH₂CH₂COOH) + NH₃. Overall: NH₂CH₂CN + 2H₂O → NH₂CH₂COOH + NH₃. The reaction is accelerated by mild acid (protonation of nitrile nitrogen), heat, or UV radiation. Clay surfaces may template the hydrolysis by activating water molecules.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to carboxylic acids is the completing step of the Strecker synthesis. In Miller's original experiments, amino acids were recovered after acid hydrolysis of the reaction mixture, but milder conditions (warm, slightly acidic water) also drive the reaction over longer timescales (Miller 1957; Schlesinger & Miller 1983). The hydrolysis proceeds through glycinamide as an intermediate. Under prebiotic conditions, volcanic hot springs (pH 3-5, 50-100°C) would provide ideal conditions. Montmorillonite clay is included as a surface catalyst that can activate water and concentrate the aminonitrile substrate.

reactions.name: Aminomethanol formation from ammonia and formaldehyde

conditions.other: Neutral to alkaline pH; aminomethanol is a thermodynamically favorable product of NH₃ + H₂CO in aqueous solution; rapid equilibrium formation

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia (NH₃) acts as a nucleophile, attacking the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of formaldehyde (H₂CO). A tetrahedral alkoxide intermediate forms, which is protonated by water to yield aminomethanol (NH₂CH₂OH). This is a simple nucleophilic addition: NH₃ + H₂CO → NH₂CH₂OH. The reaction is spontaneous and rapid in aqueous solution. Montmorillonite clay concentrates both reactants and provides acid-base catalytic sites.
reactions.reasoning: Aminomethanol is a well-known product of NH₃ + H₂CO reaction in aqueous solution and has been identified as a key intermediate in computational studies of prebiotic glycine synthesis (Xu et al. 2024). It exists in equilibrium with its dehydration product formaldimine (CH₂=NH). The Xu et al. study highlights aminomethanol as a branching point: it can either dehydrate to formaldimine or serve as a direct precursor to glycine via further reactions. This reaction connects the hydrothermal formaldehyde pool to the formaldimine pathway.

reactions.name: Dehydration of aminomethanol to formaldimine

conditions.other: UV irradiation promotes dehydration; wet-dry cycling on mineral surfaces; formaldimine (CH₂=NH) is reactive and must be consumed quickly by downstream reactions

reactions.mechanism: Aminomethanol (NH₂CH₂OH) undergoes dehydration (elimination of H₂O) to form formaldimine (methanimine, CH₂=NH). On TiO₂ surfaces under UV irradiation, the hydroxyl group is activated for elimination. Alternatively, drying on clay surfaces during wet-dry cycles thermally drives the dehydration. The resulting formaldimine is a highly reactive imine that rapidly participates in downstream reactions.
reactions.reasoning: The dehydration of aminomethanol to formaldimine is highlighted by Xu et al. (2024) as a key step in their computationally identified glycine synthesis pathway. Formaldimine (CH₂=NH) is described as 'especially reactive toward amino acids' and has lower activation barriers than HCN-based routes for glycine formation. UV-driven or thermal dehydration on mineral surfaces provides the energy input. TiO₂ is a known UV photocatalyst present in early Earth surface environments (anatase/rutile from weathered titanomagnetite).

reactions.name: Formaldimine + formic acid coupling to glycine

conditions.other: Moderate barrier (~40-50 kcal/mol from DFT); HCOOH acts as both reactant and proton shuttle; clay and mineral surfaces may lower the effective barrier; UV may assist in overcoming the barrier; computationally supported by Xu et al. (2024)

reactions.mechanism: Formaldimine (CH₂=NH) reacts with formic acid (HCOOH) in a concerted or stepwise mechanism. The imine carbon of formaldimine attacks formic acid, forming a C–C bond. Formic acid serves as both the carboxyl source and a proton shuttle, facilitating hydrogen transfer. The product is glycine (NH₂CH₂COOH) via: CH₂=NH + HCOOH → NH₂CH₂COOH. On mineral surfaces, the barrier may be reduced through surface-mediated proton transfers and transition state stabilization.
reactions.reasoning: This pathway was computationally identified by Xu et al. (2024) as an alternative to the Strecker route. They found that formaldimine has lower barriers than analogous HCN-based pathways. The reaction has a moderate computed barrier (~40-50 kcal/mol), but HCOOH acts as both reactant and proton shuttle, which is mechanistically elegant. While this route lacks direct experimental validation, it is thermodynamically favorable and the intermediates (formaldimine, formic acid) are both prebiotically plausible. Mineral surfaces (clay, phosphate) are proposed to lower the barrier through surface catalysis. This represents an important alternative to the Strecker route that does not require HCN.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of glycolaldehyde to ethanolamine

conditions.catalyst: magnetite and mackinawite surfaces; Fe²⁺ provides reducing equivalents
conditions.other: Alkaline pH ~9-10; H₂-rich hydrothermal fluid; NH₃ from vent fluid or dissolved NH₄⁺; analogous to biological reductive amination of aldehydes

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde (HOCH₂CHO) condenses with ammonia (NH₃) at the aldehyde group to form an imine intermediate (HOCH₂CH=NH). This imine is then reduced by H₂ (dissociated on the mineral surface) or by Fe²⁺-donated electrons to yield ethanolamine (HOCH₂CH₂NH₂). The hydroxyl group is preserved. Overall: HOCH₂CHO + NH₃ + H₂ → HOCH₂CH₂NH₂ + H₂O. Magnetite provides the catalytic surface for H₂ activation; mackinawite may provide additional reducing power via sulfide-mediated electron transfer.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction provides the prebiotically plausible synthesis route for ethanolamine that was missing from prior network versions. Reductive amination of aldehydes is a well-established reaction on iron mineral surfaces under hydrothermal conditions. Glycolaldehyde, produced by formose chemistry (rxn_004), is a C₂ aldehyde that can be aminated in the same manner as glyoxylate or pyruvate. The product ethanolamine then feeds into the Zhang et al. (2017) glycine synthesis route (rxn_014). Magnetite-catalyzed reductive amination is analogous to the ferroan brucite-catalyzed glyoxylate amination (Chimiak et al. 2024), extending the same chemistry to a different substrate.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal oxidation of ethanolamine to glycine

conditions.catalyst: Fe, Ni, Co, Cu metal powders
conditions.other: pH ~9.7 (alkaline); anaerobic; simulated Archean alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions; glycine formed rapidly and efficiently; minutes to hours timescale

reactions.mechanism: Ethanolamine (HOCH₂CH₂NH₂) is oxidized on metal catalyst surfaces. The proposed mechanism involves: (1) Dehydrogenation of the hydroxyl end to an aldehyde, forming aminoacetaldehyde (NH₂CH₂CHO); (2) Further oxidation of the aldehyde to a carboxylic acid, or intramolecular rearrangement yielding glycine (NH₂CH₂COOH). Metal surfaces (Fe, Ni, Co) activate C–H bonds and facilitate the dehydrogenation/oxidation steps. The high pH maintains the amine group in its unprotonated (reactive) form. Overall: HOCH₂CH₂NH₂ + [O] → NH₂CH₂COOH + 2H.
reactions.reasoning: Zhang et al. (2017) demonstrated efficient glycine synthesis from ethanolamine under simulated Archean alkaline vent conditions (80-160°C, pH ~9.7, 0.1-8 MPa) with Fe/Co/Ni/Cu metal catalysts. The reaction was described as 'rapid and efficient' with glycine appearing in minutes to hours. The metal powders simulate native metals found in serpentinizing ultramafic systems. This is a non-Strecker, non-HCN route to glycine that uses the C₂ skeleton of ethanolamine directly. The route is significant because it operates under conditions realistic for Archean alkaline vents.

reactions.name: Direct mineral surface glycine synthesis from H₂CO + CO + NH₃ on forsterite

conditions.other: Dry mineral surface; all three reactants co-adsorbed; single-barrier exergonic process; bypasses the kinetically blocked Strecker route; computationally demonstrated on forsterite (olivine) surfaces

reactions.mechanism: On the forsterite (Mg₂SiO₄) surface, H₂CO, CO, and NH₃ co-adsorb. The reaction proceeds through a concerted or near-concerted mechanism: NH₃ attacks formaldehyde to form an aminomethanol-like surface species, which then inserts CO to form the C–C bond yielding glycine. The silicate surface provides Mg²⁺ Lewis acid sites that activate CO for insertion, and surface hydroxyl groups facilitate proton transfers. The overall process is exergonic with a single kinetic barrier, unlike the multi-step Strecker route which has a prohibitively high barrier on the same surface. H₂CO + CO + NH₃ → NH₂CH₂COOH.
reactions.reasoning: Mates-Torres & Rimola (2026) demonstrated computationally that on forsterite (Mg₂SiO₄) surfaces, formaldehyde, CO, and NH₃ react to form glycine in a single exergonic step with a low kinetic barrier. In contrast, the Strecker route (HCN + NH₃ + H₂CO) is predicted to be kinetically blocked on the same surface. This route is significant because forsterite is the primary mineral in olivine, which is abundant in ultramafic rocks undergoing serpentinization at hydrothermal vents. While experimentally unvalidated, the computational evidence is strong and the pathway uses the simplest possible C₁ precursors.

reactions.name: Nitrate-mediated glycine synthesis from glyoxylate on ferroan brucite

conditions.other: pH 8-9; Fe(II) first reduces NO₃⁻ to NH₃ in situ, then NH₃ reductively aminates glyoxylate; <3% initial yield in solution but ~97% of glycine mineral-bound; weeks-long reaction; relevant to mildly oxidized early atmospheres that generated NO₃⁻

reactions.mechanism: A two-phase integrated reaction on ferroan brucite: (1) Nitrate reduction: NO₃⁻ is reduced to NH₃ by Fe(II) in the brucite lattice (NO₃⁻ + 8Fe²⁺ + 10H⁺ → NH₄⁺ + 8Fe³⁺ + 3H₂O). This is a multi-electron reduction requiring the concerted action of multiple Fe(II) centers. (2) Reductive amination: The in situ-generated NH₃ then reacts with glyoxylate through the same imine intermediate as rxn_006, yielding glycine. The mineral surface mediates both reactions, coupling nitrogen reduction to carbon amination. Most glycine product (~97%) remains adsorbed to the mineral surface.
reactions.reasoning: Chimiak et al. (2024) demonstrated this integrated pathway: ferroan brucite couples nitrate reduction with glyoxylate amination to produce glycine. This is significant because it does not require pre-existing free NH₃—instead, it uses NO₃⁻ as the nitrogen source. Initial solution yields were <3%, but acid digestion of the mineral revealed that ~97% of glycine was mineral-bound, suggesting standard analyses dramatically underestimate yields. The slower kinetics (weeks vs. the NH₃ system's faster reaction) reflect the rate-limiting NO₃⁻ → NH₃ reduction step. This pathway is relevant to scenarios where the early atmosphere was mildly oxidized, generating NO₃⁻ via lightning-produced NOₓ.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal CO₂ fixation → glyoxylate → reductive amination to glycine (NH₃)
pathways.description: Complete hydrothermal pathway from CO₂ through formate, formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and glyoxylate to glycine via reductive amination with NH₃ on ferroan brucite. This is the longest pathway, traversing multiple hydrothermal and surface environments, and converging at the glyoxylate hub for the final amination step (Chimiak et al. 2024).

pathways.name: Miller-Urey discharge → Strecker synthesis → glycine
pathways.description: Classic atmospheric discharge route: CH₄ + NH₃ + H₂O → HCN + H₂CO via lightning, followed by Strecker condensation to aminoacetonitrile and hydrolysis to glycine. This is the most historically significant pathway (Miller 1953) and operates entirely in the surface environment.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formaldehyde + atmospheric HCN → Strecker → glycine
pathways.description: Cross-environment pathway combining hydrothermal CO₂ reduction (to formate, then formaldehyde) with atmospheric/surface-derived HCN. The Strecker synthesis proceeds in surface pools where hydrothermal effluent mixes with atmospheric fallout. This demonstrates environmental interconnection: hydrothermal H₂CO meets surface HCN.

pathways.name: Formaldimine + hydrothermal formic acid → glycine (Xu et al. pathway)
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO₂ reduction provides formic acid; surface formaldehyde (from hydrothermal or atmospheric source) reacts with NH₃ to form aminomethanol, which dehydrates to formaldimine. Formaldimine then couples with formic acid on mineral surfaces to yield glycine. Based on the computational pathway of Xu et al. (2024).

pathways.name: Hydrothermal ethanolamine synthesis and oxidation → glycine (Zhang et al. route)
pathways.description: CO₂ is reduced to formaldehyde via formate (hydrothermal), dimerized to glycolaldehyde (surface), reductively aminated to ethanolamine (hydrothermal), then oxidized to glycine on metal catalysts under alkaline vent conditions (Zhang et al. 2017). This is the complete ethanolamine pathway with full provenance for all intermediates.

pathways.name: Direct forsterite surface synthesis: H₂CO + CO + NH₃ → glycine
pathways.description: CO₂ is reduced to CO (reverse water-gas shift on magnetite) and to formaldehyde (via formate reduction). Both products plus NH₃ co-adsorb on forsterite (olivine) surfaces in hydrothermal systems, where they undergo a single-step exergonic reaction to glycine (Mates-Torres & Rimola 2026). The shortest pathway from CO₂ to glycine.

pathways.name: Nitrate + glyoxylate integrated pathway on ferroan brucite
pathways.description: Glyoxylate (produced from glycolaldehyde oxidation) reacts with nitrate (from atmospheric NOₓ) on ferroan brucite, which couples NO₃⁻ reduction to NH₃ with glyoxylate amination in a single mineral system (Chimiak et al. 2024). This pathway does not require free NH₃, making it relevant to weakly oxidized atmospheres.

pathways.name: Miller-Urey H₂CO → formaldimine + hydrothermal formate → glycine
pathways.description: Atmospheric discharge produces formaldehyde, which reacts with NH₃ to form aminomethanol, then formaldimine. Separately, hydrothermal CO₂ reduction produces formate/formic acid. The two environmental products merge when formaldimine couples with formic acid to yield glycine. A cross-environment variant of the Xu et al. pathway.

pathways.name: Miller-Urey H₂CO → glycolaldehyde → glyoxylate → glycine (NH₃)
pathways.description: Atmospheric formaldehyde (from Miller-Urey discharge) undergoes formose aldol condensation to glycolaldehyde in surface pools, is oxidized to glyoxylate in hydrothermal settings, then reductively aminated to glycine with NH₃ on ferroan brucite. This pathway routes atmospheric products through the glyoxylate network.

pathways.name: Miller-Urey H₂CO → glycolaldehyde → ethanolamine → glycine
pathways.description: Atmospheric formaldehyde dimerizes to glycolaldehyde (surface formose chemistry), which is then reductively aminated to ethanolamine in hydrothermal systems, followed by oxidation to glycine on metal catalysts at alkaline vent conditions (Zhang et al. 2017). An alternative route to ethanolamine starting from atmospheric rather than hydrothermal carbon.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong pathway directly supported by Chimiak et al. 2024 for the terminal reductive amination step (~52-69% yield with NH3). The weakest link is rxn_002 (formate → formaldehyde via FTT reduction), which is thermodynamically uphill and poorly documented experimentally at realistic conditions. The formose step (rxn_004) should specify a mineral catalyst (e.g., borate or clay). Overall, the hydrothermal-to-surface-to-hydrothermal flow is well-justified.
suggestions: Specify mineral catalyst for rxn_004 (e.g., borate minerals for formose chemistry)
Consider alternative formaldehyde sources besides FTT reduction of formate
Note that rxn_006 yields are well-constrained experimentally: ~52-69% with NH3 on ferroan brucite at 20°C

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Classic Miller-Urey/Strecker pathway with strong experimental support dating to 1953. All reactions are well-characterized with known yields (0.1-5% carbon yield in discharge experiments). The pathway is entirely surface-based, which slightly lowers environmental progression score since there is no hydrothermal-surface integration. The hydrolysis step (rxn_009) requires prolonged aqueous conditions or mild acid, which is geologically plausible in evaporating pools.
suggestions: Could benefit from specifying mineral catalysts for the Strecker step
Note that HCN tends to polymerize into tar, competing with Strecker route

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Convergent pathway combining hydrothermal formaldehyde (rxn_001/002) with atmospheric HCN (rxn_007) into Strecker chemistry. Demonstrates good cross-environment integration. The rxn_002 bottleneck applies here as well. The convergence of hydrothermal and atmospheric products into a surface reaction is chemically and geologically well-motivated.
suggestions: Clarify geographic/physical scenario where hydrothermal formaldehyde meets atmospheric HCN

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Formaldimine-formic acid coupling pathway attributed to Xu et al. 2024. Literature notes moderate barriers (~40-50 kcal/mol) for the rxn_012 step, which could be kinetically limiting without appropriate catalysis. The aminomethanol → formaldimine dehydration (rxn_011) is plausible under surface drying conditions. Good hydrothermal→surface environmental flow. The formic acid from hydrothermal rxn_001 feeding into surface rxn_012 is a nice convergent feature.
suggestions: Specify whether a mineral catalyst or UV radiation assists rxn_012
Note the barrier height from Xu et al. computations and discuss whether thermal conditions suffice

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Ethanolamine route supported by Zhang et al. 2017. The terminal oxidation (rxn_014) is experimentally validated at 80-160°C, pH ~9.7 with Fe/Co/Ni/Cu catalysts. The ethanolamine synthesis (rxn_013) via reductive amination of glycolaldehyde is plausible but less experimentally validated for prebiotic conditions. The network correctly resolves the orphan intermediate problem by synthesizing ethanolamine from glycolaldehyde. The rxn_002 and rxn_004 bottlenecks propagate here.
suggestions: Specify metal catalyst for rxn_013
Note that Zhang et al. conditions (80-160°C, pH 9.7, metal powders) are very specific

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Direct forsterite surface synthesis based on Mates-Torres & Rimola 2026 computational study. The single-step rxn_015 (H2CO + CO + NH3 → glycine) is supported by DFT simulations showing a low-barrier exergonic process on forsterite. However, this is computationally predicted rather than experimentally verified, which lowers confidence. The pathway efficiently combines CO from reverse water-gas shift (rxn_003) and formaldehyde from FTT (rxn_002). Clean and short pathway.
suggestions: Flag that rxn_015 is based on computational predictions (DFT), not experimental validation
Consider whether forsterite surfaces would be available in hydrothermal settings

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Nitrate-glyoxylate integrated pathway on ferroan brucite (Chimiak et al. 2024). Experimentally validated but with initially low yields (<3%), though up to 97% of glycine was mineral-bound and recoverable only by acid digestion. The pathway is important for scenarios with oxidizing atmospheres lacking free NH3. The rxn_016 correctly captures the coupled NO3- reduction and glyoxylate amination. Multiple weak early steps (rxn_002, rxn_004) propagate uncertainty.
suggestions: Note that glycine yields were <3% initially in the NO3- system, increasing over weeks
Emphasize the mineral-binding phenomenon: ~97% of glycine adsorbs to brucite

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Convergent pathway combining Miller-Urey-derived formaldimine with hydrothermal formate. Excellent example of environmental integration: atmospheric discharge produces formaldehyde/NH3 (surface), which forms formaldimine, then couples with hydrothermal formic acid. The rxn_012 barrier issue persists. This pathway nicely demonstrates the network's convergent design, with two independent environmental sources feeding the final step.
suggestions: Specify conditions for the surface formaldimine-formate coupling
Consider whether wet-dry cycles could facilitate rxn_011 dehydration

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Clean pathway from Miller-Urey formaldehyde through glycolaldehyde and glyoxylate to glycine via reductive amination. Avoids the problematic rxn_002 by sourcing formaldehyde from discharge (rxn_007). Terminal step (rxn_006) is among the best-supported reactions in the network (Chimiak et al. 2024, ~52-69% yield). Good surface→hydrothermal environmental progression. One of the strongest pathways in the network.
suggestions: Specify mineral for formose reaction (rxn_004)
This is among the most experimentally grounded pathways - consider highlighting it

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Alternative ethanolamine route sourcing formaldehyde from Miller-Urey discharge rather than hydrothermal FTT. Avoids the rxn_002 bottleneck. The ethanolamine synthesis step (rxn_013) remains the weakest link, as reductive amination of glycolaldehyde to ethanolamine under prebiotic conditions is less well-documented than the terminal oxidation to glycine (Zhang et al. 2017).
suggestions: Provide experimental evidence for rxn_013 under prebiotic conditions
Specify the mineral catalyst for glycolaldehyde → ethanolamine reductive amination

evaluation.network_feedback:
This is an excellent and comprehensive synthesis network for prebiotic glycine. The network demonstrates outstanding literature alignment, incorporating all major known routes: Strecker synthesis (Miller 1953), reductive amination of glyoxylate on ferroan brucite (Chimiak et al. 2024), ethanolamine oxidation (Zhang et al. 2017), forsterite surface catalysis (Mates-Torres & Rimola 2026), and formaldimine-formate coupling (Xu et al. 2024). The 10 pathways, 5 hub molecules, and 5 convergence points create a robustly interconnected topology that reflects genuine chemical redundancy.

Strengths: (1) Exceptional literature grounding with proper attribution to experimental studies. (2) Well-designed hub molecules (NH3, HCOOH, CH2O, glycolaldehyde, glyoxylate) that create meaningful cross-pathway connectivity. (3) Good environmental integration with hydrothermal→surface flow and mineral catalysts specified for most reactions. (4) The ethanolamine orphan intermediate problem is correctly addressed by synthesizing it from glycolaldehyde via reductive amination. (5) Multiple terminal steps to glycine provide robust convergence.

Weaknesses: (1) rxn_002 (formate → formaldehyde via FTT) is thermodynamically challenging and propagates as a bottleneck through 6 of 10 pathways. Pathways P2, P8, P9, P10 that bypass it are stronger. (2) rxn_004 (formose aldol) lacks a specified mineral catalyst despite being labeled 'Surface'. (3) rxn_012 (formaldimine + formic acid) has high barriers (~40-50 kcal/mol) without clear catalyst specification. (4) rxn_013 (glycolaldehyde → ethanolamine) is less experimentally validated. (5) No explicit biochemical environment stage, though this is less critical for a simple amino acid target.

Overall, the network is well-designed, scientifically rigorous, and strongly aligned with the prebiotic chemistry literature. It is approved.
